game_name: Musya: The Classic Japanese Tale of Horror

element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Player learns health management before first threats
Players acquire up to 3 spells throughout game; new spells unlock progression
Descending structure mirrors narrative goal (descend to abyss)

typical_relief_triggers: Boss defeat (grants new spell, narrative progression)
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Horror-themed action with escalating difficulty; boss encounters punctuate exploration segments

difficulty_curve.early_game: Introduction to health/lives mechanics in Tengumura Village before cavern descent begins
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Escalating enemy encounters and boss battles within Tengumura Cavern as player descends
difficulty_curve.late_game: Continued descent to abyss with progressively challenging combat
notable_spikes: First boss battle (establishes danger level and spell acquisition mechanic)
Subsequent boss encounters (require mastery of newly acquired spells)

notable_level_patterns.description: Game uses Tengumura Village as safe hub/exposition space, then transitions into Tengumura Cavern for main platforming and combat progression. Mirrors narrative structure (rescue mission requiring descent).

Defeating bosses unlocks new spells (scrolls), creating distinct progression gates. Each boss defeat is narratively signaled with 'Onryō Chōfuku' (Vengeful Ghost Submitted) text, establishing monster-slaying as core loop.

sources_used: wikipedia: Musya (1992 video game) - Gameplay section, Plot section
notes: Source material (Wikipedia article) is heavily focused on localization differences, reception, and plot rather than detailed level design documentation. Element introduction order and spatial structure inferred from gameplay mechanics and narrative structure described. Specific tension rhythm cycles, dead-end frequency, and backtracking patterns not documented in available source. T3 data tier appropriate given qualitative nature of source.
